name: Ranginui
description: Ranginui, also known as Rangi or Papa-Rangi, is the Sky Father in Māori mythology and in the traditions of other Polynesian cultures. He is a primordial being who, along with his wife Papatūānuku (the Earth Mother), originally clung together in a tight embrace, plunging the world into darkness. Their many children, the gods of various natural phenomena, lived in the cramped space between them, longing for light and freedom. After much discussion and planning, Tāne Mahuta, god of forests and birds, eventually forced his parents apart, pushing Ranginui upwards to become the sky and Papatūānuku downwards to become the earth. This act brought light into the world but also caused immense grief to Ranginui and Papatūānuku, whose tears formed the rain and mist that separate them. Ranginui is associated with the heavens, rain, stars, and the source of all life-giving waters. He is often invoked in prayers for rain and good fortune. His separation from Papatūānuku is a central theme in Polynesian creation myths, representing the separation of chaos and order, and the birth of the world as we know it. He represents the masculine principle and is seen as a provider and protector from above. The separation also explains the natural phenomena of rain as his tears and dew as his sighs, a constant reminder of his longing for his wife. Stories vary across different Polynesian islands, but the core concept of Ranginui as the Sky Father remains consistent.
subtitle: The Sky Father of Polynesian Mythology

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Ranginui and Papatūānuku were locked in a tight embrace, preventing light from reaching their children. Tāne Mahuta forced them apart, creating the sky and the earth.

keyMyths.title: Ranginui's Tears
keyMyths.description: After the separation, Ranginui wept for his lost love, Papatūānuku. His tears became the rain that falls from the sky.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, The Concise Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Stars
keyMyths.description: The stars are believed to be Ranginui's eyes, watching over the world and his children below. They serve as guides and reminders of his presence.
keyMyths.source: Reed, Myths and Legends of Maoriland

sources.author: Sir George Grey
sources.description: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.
sources.text: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ranginui remains a significant figure in Māori culture, symbolizing the sky and the connection between the heavens and the earth. His story is often referenced in art, literature, and educational contexts to promote understanding of Māori cosmology and cultural values. He also features prominently in contemporary Māori art and performance.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the beginning, Ranginui and Papatūānuku existed in Te Kore, the void, before time and space were fully formed. They were the primordial parents from whom all life would spring. Their union was one of intense love and closeness, so much so that they remained locked in an eternal embrace. This embrace prevented light from entering the world, plunging their offspring into perpetual darkness. The children, who were themselves gods, grew increasingly frustrated with their confined existence. This primordial state represents the foundation of the world, a time before separation and differentiation.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The central myth involving Ranginui revolves around the separation of himself and Papatūānuku. Their children, including Tāne Mahuta (god of the forest), Tangaroa (god of the sea), and Tūmatauenga (god of war), plotted to force their parents apart to bring light into the world. After several failed attempts by other siblings, Tāne Mahuta succeeded in pushing Ranginui upwards, creating the sky and allowing light to flood the earth. Ranginui and Papatūānuku grieved intensely at their separation. The mists and clouds are Ranginui's tears of sorrow for his lost love. Tūmatauenga, angered by his brothers' actions which caused their parents pain, sought vengeance, leading to conflict and the establishment of human dominion over the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Ranginui's legacy extends far beyond a simple creation myth. He embodies the sky, the heavens, and the source of all that comes from above, including rain, wind, and celestial guidance. His separation from Papatūānuku is a foundational story that explains the structure of the universe and the relationship between the sky and the earth. In Māori art and oratory, Ranginui is often invoked to represent the spiritual realm and the connection to ancestors. The story of Ranginui and Papatūānuku continues to resonate as a powerful symbol of love, sacrifice, and the origins of the world.

shortDescription: Ranginui is the Sky Father in Māori mythology. He embraced Papatūānuku so tightly their children lived in darkness until they pushed them apart.
